articles.headline: Union Society of Albany, Troy and Vicinity -- founding circular
articles.summary: Circular "To the Colored People of Albany, Troy, Lansingburgh, Waterford, Schenectady, Greenbush and Vicinity" announcing the formation, at a meeting in Troy on the afternoon of March 9 1837, of the 'Union Society of Albany, Troy and Vicinity, for the improvement of the colored people in Morals, Education, and the Mechanic Arts.' Calls a general meeting for Monday evening, March 20, 1837, at 6 o'clock in the African Baptist Church, Albany, to organize and elect officers. Signed by Nathaniel Paul, John Johnson, Andrew Harris, Daniel Vandevere, William Rich, Michael Douge, Alex. Tuley, Geo. H. Baltimore, Richard Thompson, James Hubbard, and James Williams; resolved the circular be published in the Troy Daily Whig and Albany Evening Journal. Andrew Harris, Sec'ry, Troy, March 1837.

articles.headline: Union Society organizing meeting, Albany, March 20 1837
articles.summary: Report of the general meeting held per the circular: called to order by Rev. Nathaniel Paul and Andrew Harris; address of over half an hour by Wm. Harris on education and attention to the mechanic arts; adoption of a constitution (full text printed) establishing the Union Society of Albany, Troy and vicinity, with dues, a Board of Managers, quarterly meetings, and treasury deposits in the Troy Savings Bank.

articles.headline: Union Society officers elected
articles.summary: Officers elected for the new Union Society of Albany, Troy and vicinity: Nathaniel Paul, President; John Wandell, Vice-President (Schenectady); Benjamin Laurier, Vice-President (Albany); James Hubbard, Vice-President (Troy); Recording Secretary (Troy); Michael, Albany, Corresponding Secretary; Alex. Thurey (Thury), Troy, Treasurer. Directors listed include Samuel Struter, William Rich, Rev. D. W. Vandevere, R. P. G. Wright, and Priestly Way. Resolved that Wm. Rich and Andrew Harris be a Committee to forward proceedings to the Colored American, Emancipator, Friend of Man, and Liberator; adjourned to meet in Schenectady on the second Monday in June. Wm. Rich, Chairman of the Publishing Committee, Troy, March 29 1837.

articles.headline: On Colored Schools -- correspondent's report
articles.summary: Original communication responding to an earlier 'Importance of well qualified teachers of Colored Youth' piece; praises a flourishing Sabbath school under the superintendence of a Mr. Tappan, and urges teachers to master interesting modes of conveying scripture lessons and to cultivate sacred music instruction among students.

articles.headline: Roger Williams' (Baptist) Anti-Slavery Society -- adjourned meeting, March 30 1837
articles.summary: Adjourned meeting held in Phenix Hall; L. Tappan called to the Chair, S. Angell appointed Secretary; a committee-drafted constitution for a 'Baptist Anti-Slavery Society, Auxiliary to the American Anti-Slavery Society' was read and adopted article by article. Officers elected: J. T. Raymond, President; L. Octon, Treasurer. Board of Managers: S. Angell, R. W. Roberts, Wm. Thompson, and D. Copeland. Resolved to send delegates (J. T. Raymond and J. M. Horner) to the American Anti-Slavery Society's approaching anniversary; resolved to observe a monthly concert of prayer for the enslaved on the last Monday evening of each month.

articles.headline: The Slave Case of William Dixson (Dickson) continued
articles.summary: Continuation of the case of William Dixson, alleged slave of Dr. W. T. Allender of Baltimore; further hearing postponed a week; testimony so far indicates Dixson has lived in New York six or seven years and multiple Black and white witnesses swear he was never Allender's slave. Editorial names 'Boudinot, Nash, and other petty officers and lawyers' engaged in 'Negro Catching' with contempt, and appeals to the crowd of Black spectators to stay away from the courts to avoid discrediting the defense.

articles.headline: Marriage notice: John Jackson and Hager Parker
articles.summary: Married by the Rev. Theodore S. Wright: Mr. John Jackson to Miss Hager Parker.

articles.headline: Ancient Abolitionism -- Moses Brown's 1773 manumission of his slaves
articles.summary: Reprint from the Philanthropist recounting the editor's visit to the venerable Quaker Moses Brown of Providence, RI (then age 97), and reproducing the full text of his 1773 instrument of manumission freeing Bonno, Caesar, Cudge, Primie, Pero, Pegg, and a share in Yarrow, Tom, Newport, and the child Phillis. Certified copy by Albert G. Green, Clerk of the Providence Municipal Court, May 23 1836.

articles.headline: Rev. Peter Williams denied passage on American vessels
articles.summary: Follow-up report that Rev. Peter Williams Jr. (rector of St. Philip's African Episcopal Church, NYC), previously refused passage to and from Europe on American packet ships because of his color, finally reached home by taking passage on a British vessel, the Union (embarked Oct 25, delayed to Nassau, N.P., arrived New York late March). Rev. Noah Worcester published remarks in the Boston Register and Observer contrasting British and American racial treatment of Williams.

articles.headline: Colored schools denied to a Presbyterian minister's children in New York City
articles.summary: Anonymous letter (a Presbyterian minister of seventeen years' standing in New York) describing being refused admission for his and others' children -- three children with combined ages over thirty-two -- to a school run under the auspices of a large Presbyterian congregation, after the presiding teacher consulted church officers who withheld consent on account of color. Announces the family is preparing to leave the city to find schooling for their children elsewhere.

articles.headline: Colored Orphan Asylum -- founding notice
articles.summary: Notice that about six months earlier an association of ladies formed in New York City for the benefit of colored orphans, to provide an asylum with food, clothing, and instruction in reading, writing, and arithmetic. A house had been purchased on advantageous terms and operations were to commence about May 1, 1837. Donations of used furniture, clothing, or bedding solicited via Mr. Ree Lockwood's Bookstore, Broadway, or Messrs. Leavitt, Lord & Co.

articles.headline: Justice Bloodgood's conduct at the Dickson trial denounced
articles.summary: Reprinting a Plaindealer report that Justice Bloodgood, during the crowd/riot attending Dickson's fugitive-slave trial, expressed regret at not bringing pistols and used profanity ('damn their souls,' wishing to shoot 'the damn niggers'). The Colored American excoriates the magistrate's conduct as a disgrace to the bench.

articles.headline: Fourth Anniversary of the American Anti-Slavery Society -- notice
articles.summary: Notice that the Fourth Anniversary of the American Anti-Slavery Society will be held Tuesday, May 9, 1837, in the Broadway Tabernacle, New York City, with a Delegates' meeting the preceding day (Monday May 8) at the Society's rooms, 143 Nassau Street. Signed by the Committee of Arrangements including Simeon S. Jocelyn.

articles.headline: Tribute to Ann G. Chapman (obituary, by L. Maria Child)
articles.author: L. Maria Child
articles.summary: Tribute to Miss Ann G. Chapman of Chauncey Place, Boston, sister-in-law of Maria Weston Chapman, who died April 4 1837; praised for her devotion to abolition and self-sacrifice. Resolutions of the Boston Female Anti-Slavery Society (Mary S. Parker, President; Martha V. Ball, Secretary) expressing sympathy with the Chapman family.

articles.headline: Mechanic Arts premium notice -- James Bird
articles.author: James Bird
articles.summary: James Bird announces premiums (to encourage the sciences and mechanic arts among people of color of both sexes) for the finest pair of stitched boots ($10), stitched shoes ($3.50), hat ($2), needlework ($10), and any new invention or improvement ($15), specimens due by April 19 for exhibition by May 1.

articles.headline: Annual Report of the Temperance Society of the People of Color of Pittsburgh and Vicinity
articles.author: Lewis Woodson, Auditor
articles.summary: Annual report of the Pittsburgh colored Temperance Society by Auditor Lewis Woodson: eleven regular monthly meetings held plus one regional convention; society subscribed to the Pennsylvania Temperance Recorder, distributed about seven dollars' worth of tracts, purchased 333 Temperance Almanacs for 1837 for distribution to Black families, and raised nearly thirty dollars from members. Estimates $25,000/year (roughly $100,000 over four years) spent by Pittsburgh's Black population on intoxicating drink, arguing this sum could instead fund schooling, churches, or a manual labor college.

articles.headline: Colored Orphan Asylum -- full officer and manager roster
articles.summary: Roster of the New York Association for the Benefit of Colored Orphans (organized about six months earlier): M. Cadwise/Cadwallader, First Directress; S. Marie, Second Directress; Anna Shotwell, Secretary; Mary Murray, Treasurer. Managers named include Rebecca Betts, Ernestine Lord, Mary Day, Isabella Donaldson, Mary Trimble, Sarah P. Underhill, Hetty King, and others. Advisers listed: William P. Mott, Robert C. Cornell, Doctor Proudfit. Editorial questions why the ladies do not instead support existing colored-run schools rather than a new separate institution.

articles.headline: John Hopper attacked by a Savannah mob -- initial notice
articles.summary: Initial notice that John Hopper, a member of the Society of Friends and son of Isaac T. Hopper, was attacked in Savannah, GA by a mob of drunken men headed by a marshal named Nash while on business, on suspicion only of being an Abolitionist; rescued/detained by the Mayor of Savannah and eventually allowed to leave. Nash is identified as the same man involved in the earlier attempt to kidnap David Ruggles in New York on Dec 31 1836.

articles.headline: Fourth Anniversary of the American Anti-Slavery Society -- delegate appointments
articles.summary: United Anti-Slavery Society met at Phenix Hall; Thomas Van Rensselaer, Chair; John J. Zuille, Secretary. Resolved that John Annin, Philip A. Bell, Thomas Van Rensselaer, Henry Graves, and James Fields be appointed to represent the Society at the Fourth Anniversary of the American Anti-Slavery Society. Committee to publish proceedings: Thomas Van Rensselaer, John J. Zuille, Simeon S. Jocelyn, Samuel E. Cornish, Wm. Yates.

articles.headline: Right of Suffrage -- editorial by 'J. Gun'
articles.author: J. Gun
articles.summary: Editorial after a New York election arguing that the disfranchisement of Black citizens under the state's property qualification is unjust and unconstitutional in principle, though technically the law; urges continued circulation of petitions for repeal.

articles.headline: Deaths reported (April 1837, various)
articles.summary: Deaths noted: Judge Piatt, resident of Schenectady; Samuel Sautters, employer; Harriet, eldest child of Mr. John Carroll, in her 7th year; Denah Hedden, aged 27; Hudson Rivers Sen., aged 33; also mention that James G. Birney, editor of the Cincinnati Philanthropist, was recently fined by an Ohio judge for harboring fugitive slaves.

articles.headline: Circular letter from Philip A. Bell and Samuel E. Cornish to Philadelphia's Black community
articles.author: Philip A. Bell and Samuel E. Cornish
articles.summary: Open letter dated New York, May 6 1837, addressed 'To our Friends and Brethren beloved: In Pennsylvania, and more especially in Philadelphia, the city of Penn, of Franklin, of Benezet, and of Rush -- Lewis, Shipley, and Bispham,' soliciting subscriptions and support for the Colored American; notes 1,250 copies already circulated among New York's friends; announces that Rev. Charles B. Ray (or, failing him, the Proprietor Philip A. Bell) will visit Philadelphia after the anniversaries to solicit support; agents Whipper (Columbia) and Bowers (Philadelphia) to receive subscriptions.

articles.headline: Amos Dresser, Rev. Mr. Ketchel, and John Hopper -- abuses of Northern men cited
articles.summary: Editorial referencing the 'savage outrages committed on Amos Dresser, the Rev. Mr. Ketchel, and our fellow-citizen, John Hopper' as evidence that Southern planters, as 'aristocrats of the worst kind,' have no respect for Northerners' rights.

articles.headline: John Hopper's narrative of the Savannah mob attack -- full first-person account
articles.author: John Hopper
articles.summary: Hopper's own detailed account, dated Savannah Jan 26 1837 and New York April 26 1837: while lodging in Savannah on business he was seized in his room by a mob of about a dozen led by Daniel D. Nash, a marshal of the City of New York (formerly of Savannah), who accused him of being an abolitionist because his father Isaac T. Hopper, brother, Arthur Tappan, Barney Corse, and David Ruggles were prominent abolitionists in New York. His trunk (containing an antislavery-adjacent Quaker pamphlet on Sierra Leone) was searched; he was nearly thrown from a window before Captain Wilteberger (hotel proprietor) intervened; the Mayor and Aldermen of Savannah eventually secured his safety and released him after finding no evidence he was an abolitionist. Names Captain Nichols of the ship Angelique (New York) for assistance in his escape. Includes Nash's own published affidavit (Savannah, Jan 30 1837) claiming he only sought to correct 'erroneous impressions' about the affair. Hopper connects Nash to an earlier attempted kidnapping of David Ruggles in New York.

articles.headline: Eulogy on the Character of William Wilberforce
articles.author: Benjamin F. Hughes
articles.summary: Eulogy on William Wilberforce (died July 29 1833) by Benjamin F. Hughes, praising his lifelong leadership of British abolition of the slave trade, his 1787 collaboration with Thomas Clarkson, his 1789 motion in Parliament, and his founding of a school at Clapham that educated African youths to demonstrate their intellectual capacity.

articles.headline: Moral Reform Society of Pittsburgh -- first annual meeting and officers
articles.summary: Report of the first annual meeting of the Moral Reform Society of the City of Pittsburgh (auxiliary to the American Moral Reform Society), held the second Tuesday in March 1837. Officers elected: A. D. Lewis, 1st Vice-President; Lewis Woodson, Corresponding Secretary. Managers: Wm. Woodruff, Orange Lewis, plus a standing committee of twelve females. Full constitution printed, noting the American Moral Reform Society originated at the fifth annual Convention of the Colored Citizens of the United States, Philadelphia, June 1 1835.

articles.headline: Reprint from Freedom's Journal (1827) on colonization -- James Forten cited
articles.summary: The editor reprints part of an 1827 Freedom's Journal editorial (from when John B. Russwurm helped edit that paper) opposing the American Colonization Society, to show that intelligent colored people have long opposed it; also cites a May 1827 Freedom's Journal letter by 'a man of color' corroborated by testimony from James Forten of Philadelphia, and notes a Philadelphia meeting of nearly 3,000 Black citizens unanimously opposed to colonization.

articles.headline: Fourth Anniversary of the American Anti-Slavery Society -- proceedings
articles.summary: Report of the Fourth Anniversary of the American Anti-Slavery Society, Broadway Tabernacle, Tuesday May 9 1837, with about four thousand present; addresses by Alvan Steward, James G. Birney, Charles C. Burleigh, Esqs., and Revs. Charles Gardner and Orin Scott; annual report read by Professor Elizur Wright. The Young Men's Anti-Slavery Society held its own anniversary the next day (Wednesday) at the Third Free Church, addressed by President Beriah Green, A. Steward, J. G. Birney, and C. C. Burleigh; annual report by Dr. Lybolt. The American Colonization Society's rival anniversary at Dr. Spring's Church was addressed by Revs. Carrol, Reynolds and Hunt.

articles.headline: Deaths: Mrs. Susan Fenwick and Miss Harriet Coles
articles.summary: In Philadelphia, Thursday May 4 1837: Mrs. Susan Fenwick, mother of Mrs. P. A. Bell, in her 60th year. Friday May 5: Miss Harriet Coles, aged 18 years.

articles.headline: Presbytery of Montrose, PA -- anti-slavery resolutions
articles.summary: The Presbytery of Montrose, PA passed a series of strong anti-slavery resolutions (all but the last carried with only Rev. David R. Gilmer dissenting): declaring slaveholding a heinous sin subject to church censure; condemning bequeathing slaves as property; condemning apprehending fugitive slaves; and resolving that any church member excluding colored people from a seat in the house of God or at the Lord's table be suspended from communion until he repents. Also references similar resolutions from the Genesee, Erie, and Chautauque Presbyteries.

events.name: Union Society of Albany, Troy and Vicinity -- founding circular
events.description: A group of Black citizens met in Troy on the afternoon of March 9 1837 and, after prayerful deliberation, formed the Union Society of Albany, Troy and vicinity for the improvement of the colored people in Morals, Education, and the Mechanic Arts, issuing a circular calling a general organizing meeting in Albany for March 20.
attendees: signer of founding circular; later elected President
signer of founding circular; later Committee/Publishing Chairman
signer of founding circular; later Vice-President, Troy

events.name: Union Society of Albany, Troy and Vicinity -- organizing meeting
events.description: General meeting of colored inhabitants of Albany, Troy, Lansingburgh, Waterford, Schenectady and Greenbush per the March 9 circular; called to order by Rev. Nathaniel Paul and Andrew Harris; address by Wm. Harris on education and the mechanic arts; constitution adopted and officers elected for the Union Society.
attendees: Director; Committee to forward proceedings to press

events.name: Roger Williams' (Baptist) Anti-Slavery Society -- adjourned meeting
events.description: Adjourned meeting of a Baptist anti-slavery society (auxiliary to the American Anti-Slavery Society); constitution adopted, officers and Board of Managers elected, delegates appointed to the American Anti-Slavery Society's anniversary.
attendees: opened meeting with prayer; delegate to AASS anniversary

events.name: Colored Orphan Asylum of New York -- founding and preparation to open
events.description: About six months before this April 22 1837 report, an association of ladies formed in New York City to benefit colored orphans; a house was purchased and operations were to commence about May 1, 1837.

events.name: Colored Orphan Asylum -- officer and manager roster published
events.description: Full published roster of the New York Association for the Benefit of Colored Orphans' directresses, secretary, treasurer, managers, and advisers.

events.name: John Hopper attacked by pro-slavery mob in Savannah, Georgia
events.description: John Hopper, son of Isaac T. Hopper and a member of the Society of Friends, was assaulted in his Savannah lodgings by a mob led by marshal Daniel D. Nash on suspicion of being an abolitionist; rescued after intervention by Savannah's Mayor and Aldermen, and eventually allowed to depart the city. Nash was also implicated in an earlier attempted kidnapping of David Ruggles in New York.
attendees: victim; son of Isaac T. Hopper
mob leader; New York City marshal, formerly of Savannah
referenced as an earlier target of Nash's attempted kidnapping in New York
named by Nash as a prominent abolitionist associate of the Hopper family
named by Nash as a prominent abolitionist associate of the Hopper family
hotel proprietor who intervened to protect Hopper
captain of the ship Angelique; assisted Hopper's escape from Savannah

events.name: United Anti-Slavery Society meeting -- delegates appointed to AASS Fourth Anniversary
events.description: The United Anti-Slavery Society met with Thomas Van Rensselaer in the Chair and John J. Zuille as Secretary, appointing delegates to represent the Society at the American Anti-Slavery Society's Fourth Anniversary.

events.name: Moral Reform Society of Pittsburgh -- first annual meeting
events.description: First annual meeting of the Moral Reform Society of the City of Pittsburgh, auxiliary to the American Moral Reform Society (which originated at the fifth annual national Convention of Colored Citizens, Philadelphia, June 1 1835); officers elected for the ensuing year.
attendees: Corresponding Secretary; also Auditor of the Pittsburgh Temperance Society

events.name: Fourth Anniversary of the American Anti-Slavery Society
events.description: Fourth annual anniversary meeting of the American Anti-Slavery Society, attended by about four thousand people; addresses delivered and the annual report read.

events.name: Young Men's Anti-Slavery Society anniversary
events.location: Third Free Church, New York City
events.description: Anniversary meeting of the Young Men's Anti-Slavery Society, held the day after the parent society's anniversary; addresses delivered and annual report read.

agents.note: New agent announced at New Haven, replacing/joining prior New Haven agent listing.
